Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To Cal. Civ. Proc. Code Section 128.7
Off Calendar Sep-11-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. MICHAEL MORGAN, ESQ. of Law Office of Michael Mogan, P.C. (Tel: 949-424-5237) appearing via CourtCall for plaintiff Veronica McCluskey;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS JEFF HENRY, DAVE WILLNER, AND SANAZ EBRAHIMI'S Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To Cal. Civ. Proc. Code Section 128.7.

Matter argued and submitted by plaintiff's counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendants' motion for sanctions pursuant to Cal. Civ. Proc. Code 128.7 is granted. The Court finds that Plaintiff Veronica McCluskey's motion to lift stay, which the Court granted by order dated August 8, 2019, was both factually and legally frivolous. In particular, the Court finds that plaintiff's contention that defendants were in "default" in the arbitration proceedings was entirely lacking in either evidentiary or legal support. In fact, as set forth in the order, and as was fully known to plaintiff's counsel, the American Arbitration Association had made a clerical error by misapplying defendants' timely fees and then, as a result, administratively closed the case. Once the AAA realized and acknowledged its mistake, it requested confirmation from plaintiff that she wanted the case reopened. Plaintiff's counsel did not respond to that repeated request by the AAA, but instead brought the frivolous motion to lift the stay, by which counsel sought to take advantage of the AAA's clerical error and her own lengthy delays in order to evade her contractual obligation to arbitrate her claims and to avoid the effect of the Court's earlier order granting defendants' petition to compel arbitration. Plaintiff's counsel now compounds his misconduct by accusing defendants' counsel of "continued attempts to commit fraud upon this Court and Plaintiff" and of "lying," among other things, accusations which the Court finds to be baseless and unprofessional. Plaintiff's contention that the Court lacks jurisdiction to grant sanctions because it previously granted defendants' motion to compel arbitration is mistaken. (See, e.g., Roberts v. Packard, Packard & Johnson (2013) 217 Cal.App.3d 822, 841 [after granting petition to compel arbitration, trial court retains jurisdiction over the case to conduct further proceedings].) Plaintiff's contention that the definition of a prevailing party in section 128.7 is unconstitutionally vague is unsupported by any authority, and is itself frivolous, as is plaintiff's request for an award of sanctions against defendants for bringing the instant motion.

Plaintiff's counsel shall pay defendants' attorneys' fees incurred in opposing the amended motion to lift the stay in the amount of $22,159.50, which the Court finds to be a reasonable amount. The Court does not believe that an award of fees incurred in bringing the initial or the instant sanctions motion is warranted.

Order submitted via e-mail by defense counsel is signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Patty Dowling, CSR# 5388, e-mail: pdowling@sftc.org (302/EPS)

APPEARANCES: Atty. MICHAEL S. MOGAN, ESQ. of Law Office of Michael Mogan, P.C. (Tel: 949-612-1868) appearing for the plaintiff;

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff Veronica McCluskey's motion to lift stay is denied. By order filed November 7, 2018, this Court granted defendants' motion to compel arbitration. Contrary to Plaintiff's contention, Defendants are not in "default" in the arbitration proceedings. Rather, the American Arbitration Association made a clerical error by misapplying defendants' timely fees and then, as a result, administratively closed the case. Once the AAA realized and acknowledged its mistake, it requested confirmation from plaintiff that she wants the case reopened. Plaintiff's counsel did not respond to that repeated request by the AAA. The Court will not allow Plaintiff to take advantage of the AAA's clerical error and her own lengthy delays in order to evade her contractual obligation to arbitrate her claims, if she wishes to pursue them.

Order signed in open court.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration Or, Alternatively, To Stay Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. Section 1281.4
Off Calendar Nov-07-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Michael Mogan, Esq. 949-612-1868 for Plaintiff on the Court's phone.
Michele Floyd, Esq. 415-504-3070 and Jacqueline Young, Esq. 415-504-3168 of Sacks Ricketts & Case for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT JEFF HENRY, DAVE WILLNER, SANAZ EBRAHIMI's Motion To Compel Arbitration Or, Alternatively, To Stay Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. Section 1281.4.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendants Jeff Henry, Dave Willner, and Sanaz Ebrahimi's ("Defendants") motion to compel arbitration is GRANTED. In addition, this case is stayed pending the conclusion of the arbitration proceedings. Defendants alleged wrongful acts occurred after Ms. McCluskey had consented to AirBnB's Terms of Service (TOS), version 6, which contains an arbitration clause. By agreeing to submit disputes about the "enforcement, interpretation or validity" of the agreement to binding arbitration, the parties clearly and expressly delegated challenges to the enforceability of the agreement, including challenges based on unconscionability, to the arbitrator. (See Aanderud v. Superior Court (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 880, 892.) Ms. McCluskey's claim that the TOS as a whole is an illegal contract is also an issue to be decided by the arbitrator. The U.S. Supreme Court in Buckeye Check Cashing, Inc. v. Cardegna (2006) 546 U.S. 440, 446-449 rejected the distinction between voidable contracts and void contracts, and held that challenges to the illegality of a contract as a whole are to be decided by the arbitrator. The Defendants may compel arbitration of the claims against them because the alleged wrongful conduct was committed within the scope of their employment with AirBnB and is related to the TOS. There is sufficient "identity of interest" between signatory AirBnB and the non-signatory Defendants such that the non-signatory Defendants should be permitted to enforce the arbitration provision. Also, the allegations against all parties are inextricably intertwined. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/SRB)
